rescue_reasoning: The policy targets end-of-life management/collection of mercury thermostats: "extend the program beyond the current January 1, 2024, sunset date and to improve overall program performance." This is consistent with product stewardship/hazardous product collection (toxic pollution avoidance) and fits Resource Efficiency even if explicit outcome tokens were not detected. The climate_screen is "explicit_self", so we keep the row despite the gate failure.
policy_statement: Amend the Mercury Thermostat Collection Act to extend the program beyond the January 1, 2024 sunset date and improve performance.
mechanism_description: The state plans to extend and improve the mercury thermostat collection program so more mercury thermostats are collected and properly managed, reducing toxic mercury releases to the environment.
primary_causal_pathway: Hazardous product diversion → safe treatment/disposal → ↓ mercury releases (co-benefit: avoids some energy/material impacts of contamination response)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Increase collection and proper end-of-life management of mercury-containing products (e.g., thermostats) instead of disposal in municipal waste streams or improper handling. (2) Mediating process: Prevents volatilization/leaching of mercury during handling, storage, incineration, or landfill conditions by routing materials to compliant hazardous-waste treatment and containment. (3) Outcome: Reduced environmental mercury contamination (air/water/soil) and associated human/ecosystem exposure; any climate impacts are indirect and typically minor compared with toxics reduction.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reduced toxic pollution to air/water/soil, which is generally not a direct or dominant driver of greenhouse-gas emissions or climate-hazard exposure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: hazardous product collection/diversion → safe management → reduced mercury pollution.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: This is not principally a GHG mitigation pathway (any avoided emissions from waste handling are incidental), not adaptation (doesn’t primarily reduce climate hazard exposure/vulnerability), and not nature-based (no ecosystem carbon/heat regulation mechanism). Among available categories, it aligns most closely with upstream materials/management policy (Resource Efficiency) even though the core benefit is toxics avoidance.  
3) Edge cases applied: If implemented specifically to reduce GHGs from waste incineration/landfilling or to recover materials at scale, Mitigation/Resource Efficiency could become more central; however for mercury thermostats the dominant intent/effect remains toxics control.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Programs targeting collection and proper management of hazardous/mercury-containing products consistently act through pollution avoidance rather than a primary climate pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary designation (e.g., minor mitigation co-benefit from avoided incineration) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Category fit is imperfect because the mechanism is primarily toxics/pollution prevention (not one of the four climate categories). Classified as Resource Efficiency as the closest available umbrella for waste-management system change, with acknowledgement that direct climate relevance is generally indirect/minor.
key_indicators: "enact legislation"; "amend the Mercury Thermostat Collection Act"; "extend the program beyond the current January 1, 2024, sunset date"; "improve overall program performance"
co_benefits: Reduced hazardous-waste handling impacts and potential minor avoided emissions from contamination response/cleanup activities.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be construed as a planning-strategy/program reauthorization, but the explicit call to “enact legislation” to amend an act supports classifying it as a regulatory mandate.
